config.prompt: Name all 8 planets of the solar system in order from the Sun

items.explanation:
Closest planet to the Sun (~0.39 AU); orbits in just 88 Earth days — shortest year of any planet
Smallest planet in the solar system; similar in size to Earth's Moon
No significant atmosphere; surface temperatures swing from −180°C to 430°C (greatest range of any planet)
Large iron core makes it the second-densest planet after Earth
Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet in 2006; Mercury is now the solar system's smallest planet

Hottest planet at ~465°C surface temperature, despite not being closest to the Sun
Runaway greenhouse effect: dense CO₂ atmosphere traps heat more than any other planet
Rotates retrograde (clockwise viewed from above North Pole), so the Sun rises in the west
A Venusian day (243 Earth days) is longer than its year (225 Earth days)
Brightest object in the night sky after the Moon; visible as the 'morning star' or 'evening star'

Only known planet with life; ~4.54 billion years old
71% of the surface is covered by water — unique among known planets
One natural satellite (the Moon), which stabilizes Earth's axial tilt and drives the tides
Plate tectonics are unique to Earth among solar system planets
The astronomical unit (AU) is defined as the Earth–Sun mean distance: ~149.6 million km

Called the Red Planet because iron oxide (rust) covers its surface
Olympus Mons is the tallest volcano in the solar system (~22 km; roughly 3× Everest's height)
Valles Marineris is the largest canyon system: ~4,000 km long and up to 7 km deep
Two tiny moons: Phobos (orbits in under 8 hours, faster than Mars rotates) and Deimos
More rovers and landers sent than any other planet; Perseverance rover and Ingenuity helicopter active as of 2024

Largest planet — more than twice as massive as all other planets combined
The Great Red Spot is a storm wider than Earth that has raged for at least 350 years
95 known moons (as of 2023); the four Galilean moons (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto) discovered by Galileo in 1610
Ganymede is the largest moon in the solar system — bigger than Mercury
Europa's subsurface liquid-water ocean makes it a top candidate for extraterrestrial life

Spectacular ring system: hundreds of thousands of km wide but only ~100 m thick in most places
Least dense planet — less dense than water; would float in a sufficiently large ocean
146 known moons (as of 2023, surpassing Jupiter); Titan (largest) has a thick nitrogen atmosphere
Titan has lakes of liquid methane and ethane at surface temperatures around −179°C
The rings are made mostly of water ice and rocky debris; likely formed within the last 100 million years

Ice giant; rotates on its side with an axial tilt of ~98°, likely caused by an ancient large impact
Each pole experiences 42 years of continuous sunlight followed by 42 years of continuous darkness
27 known moons, all named after characters from Shakespeare and Alexander Pope
First planet discovered with a telescope, by William Herschel on March 13, 1781
Blue-green color from methane in its atmosphere, which absorbs red light

Farthest planet from the Sun; one orbit takes ~165 Earth years
Discovered in 1846 by mathematical prediction (Urbain Le Verrier and John Couch Adams) before telescopic confirmation
Fastest sustained winds in the solar system: up to ~2,100 km/h
16 known moons; Triton (largest) orbits retrograde — likely a captured Kuiper Belt object
Generates more heat internally than it receives from the Sun; the heat source is not fully understood

config.prompt: Name all 5 officially recognized dwarf planets

items.explanation:
Reclassified from the 9th planet to a dwarf planet by the IAU in August 2006
Located in the Kuiper Belt; has 5 moons, the largest being Charon (~half Pluto's diameter)
New Horizons flew past in July 2015, revealing water-ice mountains and a heart-shaped nitrogen plain (Tombaugh Regio)
Smaller than Earth's Moon; diameter ~2,377 km
Discovered in 1930 by Clyde Tombaugh at Lowell Observatory in Arizona

Slightly smaller than Pluto in diameter but ~27% more massive
Located in the scattered disc, far beyond the Kuiper Belt
Its discovery in 2005 by Mike Brown's team directly triggered the debate that led to Pluto's reclassification
Named after the Greek goddess of discord and strife — appropriate given the controversy it caused
Has one moon, Dysnomia (named after Eris's daughter, goddess of lawlessness)

Fastest-rotating large body in the solar system: one day lasts only ~4 hours
Egg-shaped (oblate spheroid) due to its rapid spin
Located in the Kuiper Belt; has 2 moons (Hiʻaka and Namaka) and a ring system
Named after the Hawaiian goddess of fertility and childbirth
Surface covered in crystalline water ice, giving it an unusually bright white appearance

Second-brightest Kuiper Belt object after Pluto
Reddish-brown surface covered with frozen methane, ethane, and nitrogen
Has one known moon, provisionally nicknamed MK 2
Named after the creator deity of the Rapa Nui people of Easter Island; discovered shortly after Easter 2005
No spacecraft has visited Makemake

The only dwarf planet in the inner solar system; located in the Asteroid Belt between Mars and Jupiter
Largest object in the Asteroid Belt: ~940 km diameter, comprising ~33% of the belt's total mass
Classified as a planet (1801–1850), then as an asteroid, then as a dwarf planet (2006)
NASA's Dawn spacecraft orbited Ceres 2015–2018; discovered bright spots in Occator Crater (salt deposits from subsurface brines)
Named after the Roman goddess of grain; the word 'cereal' derives from the same root
